text: There is a precise dose-response relationship between exercise intensity and BDNF production. A 2012 study by Ferris and colleagues found that high-intensity interval training elevated BDNF levels more sharply than moderate continuous effort, but the spike was short-lived — peaking within 15 minutes post-exercise, then returning to baseline within an hour. Moderate aerobic effort, while producing a smaller BDNF surge, sustains elevated levels longer. This temporal architecture matters: a brief, intense run before a demanding cognitive task may confer a neurological window of enhanced plasticity, but the timing of that window must be respected. Biology, it turns out, schedules its own appointments.
question: According to the passage, how does high-intensity interval training differ from moderate aerobic effort in its effect on BDNF?

options.text: High-intensity training produces no BDNF elevation at all
High-intensity training produces a sharper but shorter BDNF spike than moderate effort
Moderate effort produces higher BDNF levels than high-intensity training
Both intensities produce identical BDNF elevation lasting about one hour
